data.title: Reliance tops Q1 estimates as steel demand, pricing lift results
data.description:
Reliance (RS), one of North America’s largest metals service center companies, reported results on Wednesday that beat Wall Street expectations for both revenue and earnings as higher steel and aluminum prices, rising shipment volumes and strong industrial demand boosted results.

Shares of Reliance (RS) rose 2.4% in extended trading. At Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 25% over the prior 12 months.

First-quarter net sales rose 15.5% to $4.03 billion from $3.48 billion a year earlier, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $3.91 billion.

Adjusted diluted earnings per share were $5.16, above estimates of $4.66 a share.

Net income rose to $264.9 million, or $5.10 a share, from $199.7 million, or $3.74 a share, a year earlier.

Volumes hit record levels

Reliance (RS) said tons sold reached a quarterly record of 1.67 million, up 9.4% from the fourth quarter and 2.7% from a year earlier.

“Reliance is off to a strong start to 2026, capitalizing on favorable market fundamentals with first quarter volumes, pricing and earnings exceeding our expectations,” Chief Executive Karla Lewis said in the earnings release.

Average selling price per ton climbed 12.6% year over year to $2,414, supported by stronger carbon steel, aluminum and stainless pricing.

Margins expand, cash returned to shareholders

Pretax income rose 33% to $349.5 million. Gross profit margin was 29.1%, compared with 29.7% a year earlier.

Reliance (RS) reported earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization metrics showing leverage remained conservative, with net debt-to-ebitda of 1.0 times at quarter end.

The company returned about $301 million to shareholders during the quarter through dividends and share repurchases, including $234 million of buybacks.

Second quarter outlook solid

Reliance (RS) forecast second-quarter adjusted earnings of $5.15 to $5.35 a share, supported by healthy demand, higher mill prices and early shipments under a newly awarded U.S. border wall steel contract.

The company said it expects tons sold to rise 1% to 3% sequentially in the second quarter.

Reliance (RS) processes and distributes carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminum, brass, copper and specialty metals to customers in construction, aerospace, defense, energy and manufacturing. Its best-known operating companies include AMI Metals, Admiral Metals, Feralloy and Metals USA.

data.title: Medpace falls despite Q1 beats; 2026 guidance ranges include consensus
data.description:
• Medpace Holdings (MEDP) is down ~19% in after-hours trading Wednesday even though Q1 financial results beat on both lines.
  • Revenue of $706.6M beat by ~$9M, while GAAP EPS of $4.28 crushed the consensus by $0.40.
  • The decline could be the result of an announcement that President Jesse Geiger will retire, effective May 31. A news release noted that his departure is not because of any disagreements with the company. CEO August Troendle will become temporary president until a successor is found.
  • Medpace set 2026 revenue guidance of $2.755B-$2.855B. Consensus is $2.81B. Diluted GAAP EPS is forecasted to be $16.68-$17.50. Non-GAAP EPS consensus is $16.68-$17.50.

data.title: Gold and silver gain but look vulnerable to downturn, analyst says
data.description:
Gold futures edged higher on perceived bargain-hunting Wednesday after falling to a two-week low in the previous session, as investors await the next developments in the Iran war.

Iran reportedly seized two cargo ships in the Strait of Hormuz, while President Trump said the U.S. blockade of Iran's ports would continue, with no sign of peace talks restarting.

"Trump’s extension of the ceasefire reduces the immediate risk of military escalation—and with it the threat of a further inflationary oil price spike—while also weighing on the dollar," Saxo Bank analysts said in a note.

"Until a clearer path toward a peace deal emerges, gold and silver are likely to remain in competition with the dollar for direction, leaving prices rangebound for now," the bank wrote.

"Gold prices are consolidating below key resistance levels, reflecting a technical structure that mirrors earlier 2026 breakdown patterns," and silver prices are showing a pattern that could portend a "sharp directional move," Razan Hilal of Forex.com said in a note.

Hilal said a drop in gold to $4,640/oz could trigger a further slide, with that boundary being $75/oz for silver.

Gold and silver futures both snapped two-day losing streaks, with front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery up 0.7% to $4,732.50/oz and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) up 1.9% to $77.893/oz.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Packaging Corp of America tops profit estimates as box demand improves
data.description:
Packaging Corporation of America (PKG), one of North America’s largest makers of containerboard, corrugated boxes and uncoated freesheet paper, reported results that beat profit expectations but came in slightly below Wall Street’s revenue target.

Its shares rose 1.6% in extended trading. At Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 10% over the prior 12 months.

First-quarter net sales rose to $2.37 billion from $2.14 billion a year earlier, missing analysts’ consensus estimate of $2.42 billion.

Excluding special items, diluted earnings per share were $2.40, topping estimates of $2.13 a share.

Net income fell to $171 million, or $1.91 a share, from $203.8 million, or $2.26 a share, a year earlier.

Strong box shipments offset cost pressures

Chairman and Chief Executive Mark Kowlzan said the company posted a record first quarter for daily shipments in its legacy corrugated operations.

“We achieved a first quarter record in shipments per day in our legacy corrugated operations,” Kowlzan said in the earnings release.

Legacy corrugated shipments per day rose 2.8% from a year earlier, while including the acquired Greif business, total shipments per day climbed 21.8%. Packaging Corp (PKG) said stronger demand and improved product mix helped offset higher freight, recycled fiber and other input costs.

Profitability improved on adjusted basis

Packaging Corp (PKG) reported earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $476.5 million, up from $418.3 million a year earlier. Excluding special items, ebitda rose to $485.5 million from $421.1 million.

Reported earnings were weighed down by charges tied to restructuring at the Wallula, Washington mill, acquisition and integration costs, and facility closure expenses.

Second quarter outlook positive

Looking ahead, Packaging Corp (PKG) forecast second-quarter earnings of $2.33 a share excluding special items, citing continued strong packaging demand, seasonal volume improvement and higher prices for containerboard and corrugated products.

The company supplies the brown corrugated boxes used widely in e-commerce, food distribution, industrial shipping and consumer goods logistics. It also is a major producer of containerboard, the paper used to make cardboard boxes, along with office and printing paper grades.

data.title: PubMatic expects Q1 revenue of $62.M; shares slide 12%
data.description:
• PubMatic (PUBM) on Wednesday reported preliminary unaudited first quarter 2026 revenue to be approximately $62.4M vs. consensus of $58.95M, with approximately $2.5 million in adjusted EBITDA. 
  • The company said that these figures exceed its previously issued first quarter 2026 guidance of $58M to $60M in revenue and $(0.5M) to $1M in adjusted EBITDA. 
  • However, the company's shares slipped nearly 12% in extended trading on Wednesday. 
  • The company said that Paulina Klimenko, chief growth officer, is stepping down to focus on her health. Chief Revenue Officer for the Americas Kyle Dozeman is departing to pursue an entrepreneurship opportunity. 
  • Paulina Klimenko will remain in her role through July 2026 to ensure a structured transition. Kyle Dozeman will remain with PubMatic through the end of May and continue in an advisory capacity thereafter, it added. 
  • PubMatic has retained Heidrick & Struggles to conduct a search for a global chief revenue officer. The company intends to consolidate all revenue-generating functions under this global chief revenue officer, positioning the organization for accelerated, long-term profitable growth across the business.
  • The company highlighted that Chief Revenue Officers Emma Newman (EMEA) and Jason Barnes (APAC) will continue to lead their respective regions.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Graco shares slide after quarterly misses, underlying weakness
data.description:
Graco (GGG), a manufacturer of fluid-handling systems and spray equipment used in construction, manufacturing and maintenance markets, reported results on Wednesday that missed Wall Street expectations on both revenue and earnings.

Shares of Graco (GGG) fell 4.2% in extended trading. At Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 8.3% over the prior 12 months.

Revenue for the first quarter ended March 27 rose 2% to $540.1 million from $528.3 million a year earlier, missing analysts’ consensus estimate of $560.7 million. Adjusted diluted earnings per share were $0.66, down from $0.70 a year earlier and below estimates of $0.74 a share.

Net income fell to $118.5 million, or $0.70 a share, from $124.1 million, or $0.72 a share, a year earlier.

Acquisitions mask organic weakness

The company said sales growth was driven by acquisitions and favorable currency translation, which together added eight percentage points of growth. That more than offset a 6 percentage point decline in organic sales. Organic growth is the increase in a company’s sales or profit from its existing businesses through higher demand, pricing, new products or market share gains, excluding boosts from acquisitions, currency changes or one-time factors.

“Sales increased 2 percent in the quarter, with contributions from acquired businesses offsetting organic revenue declines,” Chief Executive Mark Sheahan said in the earnings release.

Operating earnings fell 4% to $137.8 million as lower gross margins and higher expenses weighed on results. Gross margin pressure stemmed from unfavorable product mix, lower-margin acquired operations and tariff costs, though pricing actions offset much of the tariff impact.

Contractor unit holds up, housing still soft

Graco (GGG) said its contractor segment, which includes paint sprayers and pavement-marking systems, posted 2% sales growth as infrastructure demand remained solid. Residential construction activity, however, stayed soft.

The industrial segment grew 4%, helped by acquisitions, though profitability declined because of project timing in powder finishing systems and weaker mix. Expansion markets sales fell 4%, led by lower semiconductor-related demand in the Americas.

Full-year outlook maintained

Despite the slow start, Graco (GGG) maintained its 2026 outlook for low single-digit organic revenue growth on a constant-currency basis and mid-single-digit total growth including acquisitions.

The company said order backlog in organic businesses increased 13% from the end of 2025, suggesting customer demand improved as the quarter progressed.

Graco (GGG) is known for products such as paint sprayers, coating systems, lubrication equipment and dispensing pumps sold under the Graco brand, with strong positions in contractor, industrial finishing and fluid management markets. Its spray systems are widely used by professional painters and manufacturers.
